A large Kona low west of Hawaii began to move east.  This was followed by significant levels of tropical moisture being drawn north over the Aloha State.  Precipitation became heavy at times over the islands, especially Oahu and Molokai.  No serious injuries were reported.  Damage estimates were not available, though there were reports of buckled road pavement and urban flooding because of poor drainage.

A cold front, in combination with an upper trough, brought heavy showers, rain, and thunderstorms to parts of the Aloha State.  The systems also generated snow for the summits of Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa on the Big Island of Hawaii.  There were no reports of serious injuries.  The weather did cause flooding problems for some residents, power and phone outages, and downed trees.  The costs of the damages were not available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused the Hanalei River to rise over its banks along Kuhio Highway at the Hanalei Bridge.

A large Kona low west of Hawaii began to move east.  This was followed by significant levels of tropical moisture being drawn north over the Aloha State.  Precipitation became heavy at times over the islands, especially Oahu and Molokai.  No serious injuries were reported.  Damage estimates were not available, though there were reports of buckled road pavement and urban flooding because of poor drainage.

Dry brush was the fuel that fed a 350-acre blaze near South Point in Kau on the Big Island of Hawaii on the 27th and 28th of the month.  The blaze was near Green Sand Beach, but it did not threaten any structures.  There were no serious injuries.  The cause of the fire was under investigation.

A fire burned about 300 acres of very dry brush in Makua Valley in West Oahu on the 24th and 25th of July.  The blaze was in the Army's Makua Military Reservation.  The fire did not threaten any structures, and there were no serious injuries reported.  The cause of the fire was unknown.

A fire burned about 170 acres of dry brush on the Haleakala side of Maui Raceway Park between Puunene and Kihei in Maui's Central Valley on the 31st of the month.  No serious injuries were reported, and no structures had been threatened.  The cause of the blaze remained under investigation by county officials.

A fire scorched 500 acres of dry California grass near Poamoho Camp, just north and west of Wahiawa, in central Oahu.  The blaze threatened homes in the camp for a time as firefighters worked to contain the wildfire.  Traffic was disrupted, as well, when officials closed both Wilikina Drive and Kaukonahua Road at Kamananui Road for much of the afternoon and evening hours on the 16th.  There were no reports of serious property damage or injuries.  Fire officials believe the fire had been deliberately set.

A cold front, in combination with an upper trough, brought heavy showers, rain, and thunderstorms to parts of the Aloha State.  The systems also generated snow for the summits of Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa on the Big Island of Hawaii.  There were no reports of serious injuries.  The weather did cause flooding problems for some residents, power and phone outages, and downed trees.  The costs of the damages were not available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was toppled by thunderstorm winds.  The tree then fell on a boat and a shed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather system west of the Aloha State triggered showers and thunderstorms across the islands.  Flash flooding and heavy rain events were commonplace, as was the case earlier in the month.  No serious injuries were reported.  Damage estimates were not available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding rains caused the Hanalei River to flow over its banks along Kuhio Highway at the Hanalei Bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather system west of the Aloha State triggered showers and thunderstorms across the islands.  Flash flooding and heavy rain events were commonplace, as was the case earlier in the month.  No serious injuries were reported.  Damage estimates were not available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy showers over leeward Oahu caused flooding at the Kalaeloa off-ramp of the H-1 Freeway.  Two vehicles that had become swamped by the water had to be towed from the site.  Additionally, a few homes were flooded along Waianae Valley Road and Lahaina Street; a debris flow closed part of Kamehameha Highway; and the westbound side of Farrington Highway became flooded near Aliinui Drive, where several cars stalled in the water.

Large swells from a series of powerful lows passing far northwest and north of the Aloha State generated surf of 15 to 35 feet, with even higher sets, along the north-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; and 10 to 20 feet along the west-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Lanai, Kahoolawe, and the Big Island of Hawaii.  A large wave injured a surfer on Maui on the 11th, and waves occasionally came across roadways near the shores on the individual isles.  However, there were no reports of serious property damage.

A swell from a powerful low far northwest of Hawaii produced surf of 15 to 30 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and along the north-facing shores of Oahu and Molokai; 10 to 20 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and the Big Island of Hawaii; and 15 to 25 feet along the north-facing shores of Maui. There were no reports of serious injuries or property damage.

A brush fire blackened 1100 acres in the Olowalu area in West Maui.  About 100 people had to be evacuated from their homes on the 4th as the fire moved close to residences; though the blaze, in the end, did not damage any structures.  There were no significant injuries.  Property damage estimates were not available.  The cause of the fire was under investigation.

An 80-acre brush fire in the Hawaiian Ocean View Estates subdivision in Kau destroyed two homes, apparently vacant, before being contained.  The blaze was reported about 3 PM on the 10th.  Shortly after the fire was reported, 50 residents from 30 homes had to be evacuated as a precaution.  The blaze was officially contained at 7:20 PM.  The residents were then allowed to return to their homes by 8 PM.||Besides the two houses destroyed, two vehicles and an agricultural building were consumed by the blaze. There were no reports of serious injuries.  The fire was caused by a resident's barbecue that got out of control.  Damage estimates were not available.

A fire burned about 6200 acres of brush near Maalaea in Maui from 7-14 June.  The fire was reported around 10 PM on the 7th and was declared extinguished at 12:30 PM on the 14th.  More than 100 people had to be evacuated from their homes in the late evening of the 7th as the fire moved close to the shoreline.  On the 8th, officials closed Honoapiilani Highway for six hours because of the blaze, which stranded thousands of motorists.  Many of these people were tourists, who ended up missing their flights out of Maui.||One firefighter was treated for smoke inhalation during the event.  No significant property damage was reported.  County officials were not able to determine the cause of the fire.

A dust devil momentarily lifted several children off the ground at Makaha Elementary School in West Oahu, causing a few minor injuries.  There were no reports of property damage.  Wind gusts with the dust devil were estimated near 60 mph.

A fire scorched 200 acres of dry brush on Kamehame Ridge in Kalama Valley in Hawaii Kai on the island of Oahu.  Dozens of residents, representing around 80 households, had to be temporarily evacuated as fire crews worked to extinguish the blaze late on the 4th and into the early hours of the 5th of July.  The fire had threatened homes valued at more than $50 million.  In the end, no homes were damaged or destroyed; and there were no serious injuries.  Fire officials said the blaze was caused by an illegal, professional-grade aerial firework.

A fire blackened 235 acres of sugar cane in Paia on the island of Maui on the 5th of the month.  The blaze did not threaten any homes or other structures, and there were no serious injuries.  The losses to the Hawaiian Commercial & Sugar Company were not available.  Fire officials believe the fire was caused by fireworks.

Very dry conditions helped sustain a 500-acre brush fire in North Kona about five miles south of Puuanahulu on the Big Island of Hawaii.  The fire remained in open terrain and did not threaten any structures from late on the 4th through the early morning hours of the 6th of July.  There were no serious injuries.  However, Mamalahoa Highway (Route 190) was closed for a time between mile markers 27 and 30 as firefighters contained the fire.  The blaze may have been caused by fireworks, according to a county official.

A brush fire blackened almost 1400 acres near the US Army's Pohakuloa Training Area (PTA) on the south-facing slope of Mauna Kea on the Big Island of Hawaii.  The fire began near Mauna Kea State Recreation Area, about a quarter-mile east of the PTA main gate, on Sunday morning, August 22nd.  The blaze was difficult to contain because of its elevation - above 6000 feet - and because of the dry and breezy conditions the firefighters encountered.  The fire also forced county officials to close a portion of Saddle Road that had been affected by the blaze from Sunday until Wednesday morning, the 25th.  The blaze was contained by the end of the month.  No significant injuries or property damage were reported, though the fire destroyed critical habitat and food sources for native palila forest birds.  Officials determined that the fire had been deliberately set.

A fire burned about 50 acres of dry brush near Kailua on the island of Oahu.  The blaze was located between Kailua High School and homes in Enchanted Lake.  Flames came within 20 feet of some of the school buildings and within 100 yards of homes in Enchanted Lake.  There were no serious injuries or property damage.  Fire officials said the blaze had been deliberately set.
Unstable conditions associated with an upper trough west of the state led to heavy rain over three islands.  There were no reports of serious property damage or injuries.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of mainland visitors to Maui became stranded by rising waters on East Maui Irrigation property in the later afternoon on the 19th.  A text message from the couple to a friend on the mainland was then relayed to the Maui police.  A rescue crew from the Maui Fire Department was dispatched, and the two visitors were located just before dark and transported to safety.  There were no serious injuries to anyone involved in the incident.  Details are from the Maui News of October 21, 2010.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire blackened 130 acres of dry brush in South Kohala on the Big Island of Hawaii.  The fire was near the Anekona Estates, on the sea side of Kawaihae Road.  Officials evacuated residents for a time who were near the fire. The blaze destroyed a farm structure and damaged a residence, but there were no serious injuries.   The cost of the damages was not available.  Fire officials were not able to determine a cause of the blaze.
A well-defined dust devil moved through parts of leeward Oahu, between Kapolei and Kunia, and received a great deal of publicity; but it did not lead to any serious property damage or injuries.

Dry conditions helped sustain a brush fire near Waialua on the island of Oahu.  The blaze scorched about 100 acres, destroyed at least one unoccupied agricultural building, and damaged some crops near Thompson's Corner on the 31st of the month.  The cost of the damages was not available.  No injuries were reported.  The fire also closed a section of Farrington Highway through the afternoon hours as firefighters tried to contain the blaze.  Fire officials determined the fire had been deliberately set.
A front moving through the island chain triggered periods of heavy rain on Oahu, Molokai, and Maui.  There were no reports of significant property damage or injuries.
An upper trough over the islands produced heavy rain on Oahu and the Big Island of Hawaii, and flash flooding conditions in Kauai.  However, no serious property damage or injuries were reported.
